title: The Route Profitability Picture Most Transport Businesses Have Never Built
linkedin:
Most transport businesses can tell you their busiest routes. Very few can tell you which routes are actually making them money.

Those are different lists.

The year-end P&L at June 30 tells you whether the business made a profit overall. It does not tell you which contracts drove that result and which diluted it. For operators planning fleet deployment and contract renewals, that distinction shapes almost every decision that follows.

When a route-level margin analysis is built from twelve months of data, three patterns tend to appear.

The anchor contract: large by volume, long-established, and often priced when fuel and award rates sat at different levels. Profitable, but carrying less margin than its revenue share implies. The renegotiation usually feels riskier than it turns out to be.

The premium work that has not been deliberately priced: urgent bookings, short-lead-time freight, regional runs with limited competing capacity. These often command higher rates per kilometre but get treated as supplementary volume rather than as a segment worth protecting.

The high-toll metropolitan route: on Brisbane and Sydney corridors, tolls alone can absorb five to ten percent of a contract's revenue before any other direct cost is counted. When these routes are priced against highway freight benchmarks that do not account for their specific cost structure, the margin compression stays hidden.

EOFY is the natural moment for this work. Twelve months of fuel receipts, driver timesheets, and maintenance records are all available, before the new year's trading shifts the picture.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance #ProfitabilityAdvice
facebook:
Quick question for transport and logistics operators: do you know which of your contracts actually made you money this financial year?

Most operators know their busiest routes. Far fewer have mapped their margin at the contract level, after fuel, tolls, driver hours, and maintenance are properly applied.

On some Brisbane and Sydney corridors, tolls alone absorb five to ten percent of a contract's revenue. That does not show up in a consolidated P&L.

Our latest post walks through the three patterns that typically emerge when transport businesses run this analysis for the first time.

https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-route-profitability-picture-most-transport-businesses-have-never-built

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
New post for transport and logistics operators across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria.

EOFY is the moment when most transport businesses close the books and confirm the year's profit figure. It is also the best moment to build a picture that the consolidated P&L cannot show: which contracts actually drove your margin and which ones diluted it.

For most transport and logistics businesses, the routes that generate the most revenue are not the routes that generate the most margin. Fuel varies by route and load. Toll costs on high-frequency Brisbane and Sydney corridors can absorb five to ten percent of a contract's revenue before any other direct cost is counted. Driver hours per kilometre differ materially between urban delivery schedules and long-haul highway work. These costs are in the accounts. They are rarely assembled at the contract level where decisions about pricing, fleet deployment, and renewal conversations actually get made.

When this analysis is built from twelve months of data, three patterns tend to appear: the anchor contract that is profitable but priced below current cost conditions, the premium regional or urgent work that has not been deliberately grown, and the metropolitan route where the specific cost structure compresses margin in ways a standard rate benchmarking process does not capture.

EOFY is the right time to build this picture before the new pricing conversations begin.

Read the full post: https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-route-profitability-picture-most-transport-businesses-have-never-built
